title: Insertion sort list
description: Sort a linked list using insertion sort

examples.explanation: Step 1: Convert to array: [4, 2, 1, 3]. Step 2: Insertion sort on array. i=1: key=2, compare with arr[0]=4, 4>2 so shift 4 right: [4, 4, 1, 3], j=-1, insert key at index 0: [2, 4, 1, 3]. i=2: key=1, compare with arr[1]=4, 4>1 shift: [2, 4, 4, 3], compare with arr[0]=2, 2>1 shift: [2, 2, 4, 3], j=-1, insert: [1, 2, 4, 3]. i=3: key=3, compare with arr[2]=4, 4>3 shift: [1, 2, 4, 4], compare with arr[1]=2, 2<3 stop, insert at index 2: [1, 2, 3, 4]. Step 3: Rebuild list: create nodes 1->2->3->4.

Step 1: Array: [-1, 5, 3, 4, 0]. Step 2: Insertion sort. i=1: key=5, arr[0]=-1 <5, no shift: [-1,5,3,4,0]. i=2: key=3, compare 5>3 shift: [-1,5,5,4,0], compare -1<3 stop, insert at index 1: [-1,3,5,4,0]. i=3: key=4, compare 5>4 shift: [-1,3,5,5,0], compare 3<4 stop, insert at index 2: [-1,3,4,5,0]. i=4: key=0, compare 5>0 shift: [-1,3,4,5,5], compare 4>0 shift: [-1,3,4,4,5], compare 3>0 shift: [-1,3,3,4,5], compare -1<0 stop, insert at index 1: [-1,0,3,4,5]. Step 3: Rebuild list: -1->0->3->4->5.

approaches.name: Approach 1: Brute Force via Array Conversion O(n²)
approaches.summary: Convert the linked list to an array, sort the array using insertion sort, and rebuild the linked list. This simplifies pointer manipulation but loses the in-place advantage of linked lists.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Sort Linked List via Array Conversion and Insertion Sort
ladders.desc: Complete solution: convert linked list to array, sort array with insertion sort, rebuild sorted linked list.
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the linked list sorting problem by leveraging the simpler array data structure. The intuition is that while insertion sort on linked lists requires complex pointer manipulation, arrays provide random access that simplifies the insertion sort algorithm. We trade space efficiency (O(n) extra space) for implementation simplicity. The algorithm has three phases: 1) Traverse the linked list, storing all node values in an array. This gives us O(n) time and O(n) space. 2) Apply insertion sort to the array. Insertion sort works by maintaining a sorted subarray and inserting each new element into its correct position. For each element from index 1 to n-1, we compare it with elements in the sorted portion (to its left) and shift larger elements right until we find the correct insertion point. This takes O(n²) worst-case time when the array is reverse sorted. 3) Rebuild the linked list by creating new nodes with the sorted array values. We must handle edge cases like empty lists and single-node lists. The connection to the parent problem is direct—this is the complete solution. Common mistakes include forgetting to update pointers when rebuilding the list, not handling the head pointer correctly, and implementing insertion sort incorrectly on arrays. The time complexity is O(n²) due to the insertion sort, and space is O(n) for the array. Real code pattern: def insertionSortList(head): if not head: return None; arr = []; curr = head; while curr: arr.append(curr.val); curr = curr.next; for i in range(1, len(arr)): key = arr[i]; j = i-1; while j >= 0 and arr[j] > key: arr[j+1] = arr[j]; j -= 1; arr[j+1] = key; dummy = ListNode(0); curr = dummy; for val in arr: curr.next = ListNode(val); curr = curr.next; return dummy.next

examples.trace: Step 1: Convert to array: [4, 2, 1, 3]. Step 2: Insertion sort on array. i=1: key=2, compare with arr[0]=4, 4>2 so shift 4 right: [4, 4, 1, 3], j=-1, insert key at index 0: [2, 4, 1, 3]. i=2: key=1, compare with arr[1]=4, 4>1 shift: [2, 4, 4, 3], compare with arr[0]=2, 2>1 shift: [2, 2, 4, 3], j=-1, insert: [1, 2, 4, 3]. i=3: key=3, compare with arr[2]=4, 4>3 shift: [1, 2, 4, 4], compare with arr[1]=2, 2<3 stop, insert at index 2: [1, 2, 3, 4]. Step 3: Rebuild list: create nodes 1->2->3->4.

Step 1: Array: [-1, 5, 3, 4, 0]. Step 2: Insertion sort. i=1: key=5, arr[0]=-1 <5, no shift: [-1,5,3,4,0]. i=2: key=3, compare 5>3 shift: [-1,5,5,4,0], compare -1<3 stop, insert at index 1: [-1,3,5,4,0]. i=3: key=4, compare 5>4 shift: [-1,3,5,5,0], compare 3<4 stop, insert at index 2: [-1,3,4,5,0]. i=4: key=0, compare 5>0 shift: [-1,3,4,5,5], compare 4>0 shift: [-1,3,4,4,5], compare 3>0 shift: [-1,3,3,4,5], compare -1<0 stop, insert at index 1: [-1,0,3,4,5]. Step 3: Rebuild list: -1->0->3->4->5.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Insertion Sort on Array
ladders.desc: Sort an array using the insertion sort algorithm.
ladders.explanation: This is the core sorting component of the full solution. The intuition behind insertion sort is similar to how you sort playing cards in your hand: you maintain a sorted section and insert each new card into its correct position. For arrays, we start with the first element as sorted (trivially, a single element is sorted). Then for each subsequent element (from index 1 to n-1), we store it as a 'key' and compare it with elements in the sorted portion (to its left). We shift all larger elements one position to the right until we find the correct insertion point for the key, then place the key there. This algorithm is stable (preserves order of equal elements) and works well for small or nearly sorted arrays. Step-by-step: 1) Loop i from 1 to n-1. 2) Set key = arr[i]. 3) Set j = i-1. 4) While j >= 0 and arr[j] > key: arr[j+1] = arr[j]; j -= 1. 5) arr[j+1] = key. Time complexity: O(n²) worst-case (reverse sorted array) and best-case O(n) (already sorted). Space: O(1) extra space. Connection to level 0: This subroutine sorts the array after conversion from the linked list. Common mistakes include incorrect loop boundaries, not using a temporary variable for the key, and incorrect comparison in the while condition (should be > for ascending sort). Real code pattern: def insertionSort(arr): for i in range(1, len(arr)): key = arr[i]; j = i-1; while j >= 0 and arr[j] > key: arr[j+1] = arr[j]; j -= 1; arr[j+1] = key

ladders.title: Core Logic — Convert Linked List to Array and Back
ladders.desc: Transform linked list data structure to array for sorting, then reconstruct linked list.
ladders.explanation: This is the structural transformation at the heart of the approach. The intuition is that arrays provide constant-time access to elements by index, which simplifies algorithms like insertion sort that require comparing and shifting elements. However, linked lists only allow sequential access, making similar operations more complex. By converting to an array, we trade O(n) extra space for algorithmic simplicity. The conversion process involves two main operations: 1) Linked list to array: Traverse the linked list from head to null, collecting each node's value into a dynamic array (list in Python). This requires O(n) time and O(n) space. We use a while loop: curr = head; arr = []; while curr: arr.append(curr.val); curr = curr.next. 2) Array to linked list: Create a new linked list from the sorted array. We use a dummy node technique to simplify head handling: create a dummy node, maintain a current pointer starting at dummy, iterate through the array creating new nodes and linking them, then return dummy.next. This also takes O(n) time. Connection to level 1: After conversion to array, we apply insertion sort; after sorting, we convert back. Common mistakes include not updating pointers correctly during traversal (infinite loops), forgetting to handle empty lists, and not using a dummy node leading to edge cases with head updates. Real code pattern: def listToArray(head): arr=[]; curr=head; while curr: arr.append(curr.val); curr=curr.next; return arr. def arrayToList(arr): dummy=ListNode(0); curr=dummy; for val in arr: curr.next=ListNode(val); curr=curr.next; return dummy.next

examples.trace: Convert to array: Start with head=4. arr=[], curr=4: append 4, arr=[4], curr=2. Append 2, arr=[4,2], curr=1. Append 1, arr=[4,2,1], curr=3. Append 3, arr=[4,2,1,3], curr=null stop. After sorting array to [1,2,3,4], rebuild list: dummy=0, curr=dummy. val=1: curr.next=new node(1), curr=node1. val=2: curr.next=new node(2), curr=node2. val=3: curr.next=new node(3), curr=node3. val=4: curr.next=new node(4), curr=node4. Return dummy.next which points to node1.

Convert: head=-1, arr=[], curr=-1: append -1, arr=[-1], curr=5. Append 5, arr=[-1,5], curr=3. Append 3, arr=[-1,5,3], curr=null. After sorting to [-1,3,5], rebuild: dummy=0, curr=dummy. val=-1: curr.next=node(-1), curr=node(-1). val=3: curr.next=node(3), curr=node(3). val=5: curr.next=node(5), curr=node(5). Return dummy.next pointing to node(-1).

ladders.title: Building Block — Traverse and Collect Values from Linked List
ladders.desc: Iterate through a linked list, extracting all node values into a list.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is essential for the conversion step. The intuition is that linked lists are sequential data structures, so we must visit each node exactly once to access its data. We use a simple traversal algorithm: start at the head node, move to the next node repeatedly until we reach null (the end of the list). At each node, we record its value in a dynamic array (list). This is a linear scan with O(n) time complexity, where n is the number of nodes. The space required is O(n) for storing the values. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize an empty list 'values'. 2) Set a pointer 'current' to the head of the linked list. 3) While current is not null: append current.val to values; set current = current.next. 4) Return values. This operation is fundamental to many linked list problems where you need to process all elements. Connection to level 2: This is the first half of the conversion process. Common mistakes include infinite loops by not updating the current pointer, off-by-one errors by stopping too early or late, and modifying the original list unintentionally (though here we only read values). Real code pattern: def getValues(head): values = []; curr = head; while curr: values.append(curr.val); curr = curr.next; return values

examples.trace: Start: values=[], curr=node(4). curr not null: append 4, values=[4], curr=curr.next=node(2). Append 2, values=[4,2], curr=node(1). Append 1, values=[4,2,1], curr=node(3). Append 3, values=[4,2,1,3], curr=null. Stop, return [4,2,1,3].
Start: values=[], curr=node(-1). Append -1, values=[-1], curr=node(5). Append 5, values=[-1,5], curr=node(3). Append 3, values=[-1,5,3], curr=null. Return [-1,5,3].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Create Linked List from Array of Values
ladders.desc: Build a singly linked list given an array of values.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation is about constructing a linked list data structure. The intuition is that a linked list is a chain of nodes, where each node contains a value and a pointer to the next node. To build it from an array, we need to create nodes for each value and link them in sequence. We use a dummy node technique to simplify handling the head of the list: create a temporary dummy node, maintain a 'current' pointer that starts at the dummy, iterate through the array, for each value create a new node, set current.next to this new node, then move current to this new node. Finally, return dummy.next, which is the actual head of the new list. This ensures we don't lose track of the head and handles empty arrays gracefully. Step-by-step: 1) Create a dummy node with value 0 (or any value). 2) Set curr = dummy. 3) For each value v in array: create newNode with val=v; set curr.next = newNode; set curr = newNode. 4) Return dummy.next. Time complexity: O(n) for n values. Space: O(n) for the new nodes. Connection to level 3: After getting values from a linked list and sorting them, we use this to rebuild a sorted linked list. Common mistakes include not updating the current pointer (resulting in a list with only the last node), forgetting to handle empty arrays (should return null), and memory leaks in languages like C++ (not addressed here). Real code pattern: def createList(arr): dummy = ListNode(0); curr = dummy; for val in arr: curr.next = ListNode(val); curr = curr.next; return dummy.next

examples.trace: Start: dummy=node(0), curr=dummy. val=1: newNode=node(1), curr.next=node1, curr=node1. val=2: newNode=node(2), curr.next=node2, curr=node2. val=3: newNode=node(3), curr.next=node3, curr=node3. val=4: newNode=node(4), curr.next=node4, curr=node4. Return dummy.next which is node1.

dummy=node(0), curr=dummy. val=-1: newNode=node(-1), curr.next=node(-1), curr=node(-1). val=3: newNode=node(3), curr.next=node3, curr=node3. val=5: newNode=node(5), curr.next=node5, curr=node5. Return dummy.next pointing to node(-1).

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understand Linked List Node Structure
ladders.desc: Learn what a singly linked list node is and how to create one.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept for working with linked lists. A singly linked list node is a basic data structure that contains two parts: a value (or data) and a pointer/reference to the next node in the sequence. In Python, we typically define it as a class with attributes 'val' and 'next'. The intuition is that it's like a chain of boxes: each box holds a piece of data and a link to the next box. The last box has a link pointing to None (or null), indicating the end of the list. To create a node, we instantiate the class with a value, and by default, set next to None. For example, node = ListNode(5) creates a node with val=5 and next=None. We can link nodes by setting the next attribute: node1.next = node2. This foundation is crucial because all linked list operations—traversal, insertion, deletion—depend on manipulating these nodes and their pointers. Connection to level 4: To build a linked list from an array, we need to create multiple nodes and link them. Common mistakes include confusing nodes with values (e.g., treating node.val as the node itself), not initializing next to None, and circular references (setting next to itself or a previous node accidentally). Real code pattern: class ListNode: def __init__(self, val=0, next=None): self.val = val; self.next = next. Then node1 = ListNode(1); node2 = ListNode(2); node1.next = node2 creates 1->2.

examples.trace: Call ListNode(3): creates an object with attributes val=3, next=None. No further steps.

examples.input: Create two nodes with values 1 and 2, link them
examples.trace: Step 1: node1 = ListNode(1): val=1, next=None. Step 2: node2 = ListNode(2): val=2, next=None. Step 3: node1.next = node2: now node1's next points to node2. Result: node1.val=1, node1.next=node2; node2.val=2, node2.next=None.

testCases.input: Create two nodes with values 1 and 2, link them
Create two nodes 10 and 20, don't link
Create node with value 100 and set next to itself
testCases.expected: Node with val=100, next points to itself

testCases.input: Create node with value 7 and next as None explicitly
Create two nodes 3 and 4, link 3->4, then change 3's next to None

approaches.name: Approach 2: In-Place Insertion Sort O(n²)
approaches.summary: Iterate through the linked list, removing each node and inserting it into a sorted sublist at the correct position. This maintains the linked list structure without extra data structures.

ladders.title: Full Problem — In-Place Insertion Sort on Linked List
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of insertion sort on a singly linked list using in-place node manipulation.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is to maintain a sorted sublist that grows as we process each node from the original list. We start with an empty sorted list and iterate through the input list. For each node, we remove it from its current position and insert it into the correct position in the sorted sublist. This works because insertion sort builds the final sorted list one item at a time, and linked lists allow efficient node removal and insertion without shifting elements like in arrays. The algorithm uses two pointers: one to traverse the unsorted portion, and another to find the insertion point in the sorted portion. We need a dummy head node to handle edge cases when inserting before the first element. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Initialize a dummy node pointing to null (this will be the start of our sorted list). 2. Initialize current pointer to head of input list. 3. While current is not null: a. Store next node to process. b. Find insertion point in sorted list (node where sorted.next.val > current.val or end). c. Insert current node after insertion point. d. Move current to next stored node. 4. Return dummy.next as new head. Time complexity is O(n²) in worst case (when list is reverse sorted) as each insertion may require scanning the entire sorted sublist. Best case is O(n) when list is already sorted. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few pointers. This connects to L1 by using the node insertion subroutine repeatedly. Common mistakes include: not handling empty lists, losing reference to next node before insertion, infinite loops from incorrect pointer updates, and not using dummy node for edge cases. Real code pattern: def insertionSortList(head): dummy = ListNode(0) curr = head while curr: nxt = curr.next prev = dummy while prev.next and prev.next.val < curr.val: prev = prev.next curr.next = prev.next prev.next = curr curr = nxt return dummy.next

examples.trace:
Start: head=4->2->1->3, dummy=0->null
Step1: curr=4, nxt=2, find insertion: prev=dummy (0), prev.next=null, insert 4 after dummy: dummy=0->4, curr=2
Step2: curr=2, nxt=1, find insertion: prev=dummy (0), prev.next=4 (4>2), insert 2 after dummy: dummy=0->2->4, curr=1
Step3: curr=1, nxt=3, find insertion: prev=dummy (0), prev.next=2 (2>1), insert 1 after dummy: dummy=0->1->2->4, curr=3
Step4: curr=3, nxt=null, find insertion: prev=dummy (0), move prev to 2 (prev.next=2, 2<3), prev.next=4 (4>3), insert 3 after 2: dummy=0->1->2->3->4, curr=null
Return dummy.next=1->2->3->4

Start: head=-1->5->3->4->0, dummy=0->null
Step1: curr=-1, nxt=5, find insertion: prev=dummy (0), prev.next=null, insert -1 after dummy: dummy=0->-1, curr=5
Step2: curr=5, nxt=3, find insertion: prev=dummy (0), move prev to -1 (prev.next=-1, -1<5), prev.next=null, insert 5 after -1: dummy=0->-1->5, curr=3
Step3: curr=3, nxt=4, find insertion: prev=dummy (0), move prev to -1 (prev.next=-1, -1<3), prev.next=5 (5>3), insert 3 after -1: dummy=0->-1->3->5, curr=4
Step4: curr=4, nxt=0, find insertion: prev=dummy (0), move prev to -1 (prev.next=-1, -1<4), move prev to 3 (prev.next=3, 3<4), prev.next=5 (5>4), insert 4 after 3: dummy=0->-1->3->4->5, curr=0
Step5: curr=0, nxt=null, find insertion: prev=dummy (0), move prev to -1 (prev.next=-1, -1<0), prev.next=3 (3>0), insert 0 after -1: dummy=0->-1->0->3->4->5, curr=null
Return dummy.next=-1->0->3->4->5

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Insert Node into Sorted Linked List
ladders.desc: Given a sorted linked list and a node, insert the node at the correct position to maintain sorted order.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that to maintain a sorted list, we need to find the first node whose value is greater than the node to insert, then place our node right before it. If no such node exists (all values are smaller), we insert at the end. This works because linked lists allow O(1) insertion once we find the correct position, unlike arrays which require shifting. We use a dummy head node to simplify edge cases when inserting at the beginning. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Initialize prev pointer to dummy head. 2. While prev.next exists and prev.next.val < node.val: move prev to prev.next. 3. Insert node after prev: node.next = prev.next, prev.next = node. Time complexity is O(k) where k is the length of the sorted list (worst case scanning entire list). Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into L0 by being the core operation repeated for each node in the full insertion sort. Common mistakes include: not handling empty sorted list, inserting node without disconnecting it from its original next pointer (causing cycles), and incorrect comparison operators. Real code pattern: def insertIntoSorted(sorted_head, node): dummy = ListNode(0, sorted_head) prev = dummy while prev.next and prev.next.val < node.val: prev = prev.next node.next = prev.next prev.next = node return dummy.next

examples.trace:
Start: dummy=0->1->3->5, node=2, prev=dummy (0)
Step1: prev.next=1, 1<2, move prev to 1
Step2: prev.next=3, 3>2, stop
Insert: node.next=prev.next=3, prev.next=node=2
Result: dummy.next=1->2->3->5

Start: dummy=0->2->4, node=6, prev=dummy (0)
Step1: prev.next=2, 2<6, move prev to 2
Step2: prev.next=4, 4<6, move prev to 4
Step3: prev.next=null, stop
Insert: node.next=prev.next=null, prev.next=node=6
Result: dummy.next=2->4->6

ladders.title: Core Logic — Maintaining Sorted Sublist While Processing Nodes
ladders.desc: The technique of building a sorted list incrementally by removing and inserting nodes.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that insertion sort works by keeping the left portion sorted and extending it one element at a time. For linked lists, we can physically move nodes from the original list to the sorted portion without extra memory. This works because we maintain two logical sections: sorted (initially empty) and unsorted (original list). We iterate through unsorted nodes, detach each one, and insert it into the correct position in the sorted section. The key insight is that we don't need to copy values—we can rearrange the actual nodes. The algorithm: 1. Initialize sorted_head = null. 2. While unsorted list has nodes: a. current = first node of unsorted. b. Remove current from unsorted. c. Insert current into sorted_head at correct position. d. Update sorted_head if needed. 3. Return sorted_head. Time complexity analysis: Each insertion takes O(k) where k is current sorted size, leading to O(n²) total. Space is O(1). This connects to L1 as it uses the insertion subroutine repeatedly. Common mistakes: Not properly removing node from unsorted (losing reference to rest of list), not updating head pointers correctly, and creating cycles. Real code pattern: def buildSorted(unsorted): sorted_head = None curr = unsorted while curr: nxt = curr.next # Remove curr from unsorted curr.next = None # Insert into sorted sorted_head = insertNode(sorted_head, curr) curr = nxt return sorted_head

examples.trace:
Start: unsorted=3->1->2, sorted=null
Step1: curr=3, nxt=1, remove 3, insert into sorted (null): sorted=3, unsorted=1->2
Step2: curr=1, nxt=2, remove 1, insert into sorted (3): find position (before 3), sorted=1->3, unsorted=2
Step3: curr=2, nxt=null, remove 2, insert into sorted (1->3): find position (after 1, before 3), sorted=1->2->3, unsorted=null
Return sorted=1->2->3

Start: unsorted=5->1->4, sorted=null
Step1: curr=5, nxt=1, remove 5, insert into sorted (null): sorted=5, unsorted=1->4
Step2: curr=1, nxt=4, remove 1, insert into sorted (5): find position (before 5), sorted=1->5, unsorted=4
Step3: curr=4, nxt=null, remove 4, insert into sorted (1->5): find position (after 1, before 5), sorted=1->4->5, unsorted=null
Return sorted=1->4->5

ladders.title: Building Block — Traversing and Modifying Linked List Pointers
ladders.desc: Safely navigating a linked list while changing node connections.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that linked lists are accessed via pointers, and modifying them requires careful pointer updates to avoid losing nodes or creating cycles. This works because each node only knows its next neighbor, so we must store references before changing pointers. The key technique is using multiple pointers (like current, next, prev) to track positions. Step-by-step for safe traversal and modification: 1. Always store next node before modifying current node's next pointer. 2. Update pointers in correct order to avoid breaking the list. 3. Use dummy nodes for edge cases. Example operation: removing a node. Algorithm: to remove node curr from list: nxt = curr.next, prev.next = nxt (where prev is node before curr). Time complexity O(1) for removal if we have prev pointer, O(n) if we need to find prev. Space O(1). This feeds into L2 as we need to remove nodes from unsorted list and insert into sorted. Common mistakes: Forgetting to store next pointer before changing curr.next, updating pointers in wrong order, and not handling null pointers. Real code pattern: def removeNode(prev, curr): # Removes curr where prev is node before curr nxt = curr.next prev.next = nxt curr.next = None # Optional: disconnect return nxt

examples.trace:
Start: prev=A, curr=B, list=A->B->C->D
Step1: Store nxt = curr.next = C
Step2: Update prev.next = nxt: A.next = C
Step3: (Optional) curr.next = None: B.next = null
Result: A->C->D, B isolated

Start: Using dummy node: dummy->X->Y->Z, prev=dummy, curr=X
Step1: Store nxt = curr.next = Y
Step2: Update prev.next = nxt: dummy.next = Y
Step3: curr.next = None: X.next = null
Result: dummy.next=Y->Z, X isolated

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Finding Insertion Position in Linked List
ladders.desc: Scanning a sorted linked list to find where a new value should be inserted.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that to insert a value into a sorted list, we need to find the first node whose value is greater than our value, because we want to insert just before that node to maintain ascending order. If we reach the end (null), we insert at the end. This works because we traverse the list sequentially, comparing values until we find the right spot. The algorithm: 1. Start at head of sorted list (or dummy node). 2. While current node exists and current.value < insert_value: move to next node. 3. Stop when current is null or current.value >= insert_value. 4. The insertion point is the node before current. Time complexity O(k) where k is list length. Space O(1). This feeds into L3 as it's used when inserting nodes. Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors (inserting after instead of before), not handling empty lists, and incorrect comparison operators (using <= instead of < for stable sort). Real code pattern: def findInsertionPoint(sorted_head, value): dummy = ListNode(0, sorted_head) prev = dummy while prev.next and prev.next.val < value: prev = prev.next return prev # Node to insert after

examples.trace:
Start: dummy=0->2->4->6, value=3, prev=dummy (0)
Step1: prev.next=2, 2<3, move prev to 2
Step2: prev.next=4, 4>3, stop
Return prev (node with value 2)

Start: dummy=0->1->3->5, value=7, prev=dummy (0)
Step1: prev.next=1, 1<7, move prev to 1
Step2: prev.next=3, 3<7, move prev to 3
Step3: prev.next=5, 5<7, move prev to 5
Step4: prev.next=null, stop
Return prev (node with value 5)

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Linked List Node Structure and Traversal
ladders.desc: Understanding how linked lists are represented and how to move through them.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that a linked list is a sequence of nodes where each node contains data and a pointer to the next node. Unlike arrays, elements are not stored contiguously in memory. This works because each node knows only its immediate neighbor, allowing dynamic size and efficient insertions/deletions. The foundation concept includes: 1. Node structure: typically has 'val' (data) and 'next' (pointer to next node). 2. Head pointer: points to first node; if head is null, list is empty. 3. Traversal: start at head, follow next pointers until null. Step-by-step traversal: current = head; while current != null: process current.val; current = current.next. Time complexity O(n) for traversal. Space O(1). This feeds into L4 as all linked list operations build on this. Common mistakes: Accessing null pointer properties (causing errors), not updating current pointer in loop (infinite loop), and confusing node value with node reference. Real code pattern: class ListNode: def __init__(self, val=0, next=None): self.val = val; self.next = next. Traversal: def traverse(head): curr = head; while curr: print(curr.val); curr = curr.next.

examples.trace:
Start: head points to node with val=3, next points to node with val=1
Step1: curr=head (val=3), print 3, curr=curr.next (node with val=1)
Step2: curr (val=1), print 1, curr=curr.next (node with val=4)
Step3: curr (val=4), print 4, curr=curr.next (null)
Stop

Start: head points to node with val=5, next=null
Step1: curr=head (val=5), print 5, curr=curr.next (null)
Stop

approaches.name: Approach 3: Merge Sort O(n log n)
approaches.summary: Use a divide-and-conquer strategy: recursively split the linked list into halves, sort each half with merge sort, and merge the sorted halves. This is more efficient for linked lists due to easy merging without extra space.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Sort Linked List Using Merge Sort
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of merge sort for a singly linked list
ladders.explanation: This is the complete solution to sorting a linked list using merge sort. The intuition is that merge sort's divide-and-conquer approach works exceptionally well for linked lists because: 1) splitting a linked list into halves can be done efficiently using the slow/fast pointer technique, 2) merging two sorted linked lists is straightforward and requires only O(1) extra space (unlike arrays which need O(n) space), and 3) the recursive nature aligns perfectly with linked list traversal. The algorithm has three main components: 1) Find the middle of the list using slow/fast pointers, 2) Recursively sort the left and right halves, 3) Merge the two sorted halves. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Base case: if head is null or head.next is null, return head (already sorted). 2. Find middle node using slow/fast pointers: slow = head, fast = head.next. While fast and fast.next: slow = slow.next, fast = fast.next.next. 3. Split list: right_head = slow.next, slow.next = null. 4. Recursively sort left half: left = mergeSort(head). 5. Recursively sort right half: right = mergeSort(right_head). 6. Merge the two sorted halves using merge function. Time complexity: O(n log n) - each level of recursion processes all n nodes, and there are log n levels. Space complexity: O(log n) for recursion stack. Connection to L1: This level combines the merge function (L1) with the recursive splitting logic. Common mistakes: 1) Not handling the base case correctly, 2) Incorrect middle finding that leads to infinite recursion, 3) Not properly disconnecting the two halves when splitting, 4) Not updating pointers correctly during merge. Real code pattern: def mergeSort(head): if not head or not head.next: return head; slow, fast = head, head.next; while fast and fast.next: slow = slow.next; fast = fast.next.next; right_head = slow.next; slow.next = None; left = mergeSort(head); right = mergeSort(right_head); return merge(left, right)

examples.trace: Step 1: Input list: 4->2->1->3. Step 2: Find middle: slow starts at 4, fast starts at 2. After first iteration: slow=2, fast=1. After second iteration: slow=1, fast=null (fast.next is null). Middle is node 1. Step 3: Split: left half = 4->2->1, right half = 3. Step 4: Recursively sort left half (4->2->1): Find middle: slow starts at 4, fast at 2. After first iteration: slow=2, fast=null. Middle is node 2. Split left: left-left = 4->2, right-left = 1. Sort left-left (4->2): Find middle: slow=4, fast=2. Middle is node 4. Split: left-left-left = 4, right-left-left = 2. Both are single nodes (base case). Merge 4 and 2: result = 2->4. Sort right-left (1): single node, returns 1. Merge left-left result (2->4) with right-left (1): Compare 2 and 1: 1 smaller, result starts with 1. Compare 2 and None: add 2, then 4. Result: 1->2->4. Step 5: Sort right half (3): single node, returns 3. Step 6: Merge left result (1->2->4) with right result (3): Compare 1 and 3: 1 smaller. Compare 2 and 3: 2 smaller. Compare 4 and 3: 3 smaller. Compare 4 and None: add 4. Final: 1->2->3->4.

Step 1: Input: -1->5->3->4->0. Step 2: Find middle: slow=-1, fast=5. Iteration 1: slow=5, fast=4. Iteration 2: slow=3, fast=0. Iteration 3: slow=4, fast=null (fast.next is null). Middle is node 4. Step 3: Split: left = -1->5->3->4, right = 0. Step 4: Sort left: Find middle: slow=-1, fast=5. Iteration 1: slow=5, fast=3. Iteration 2: slow=3, fast=null. Middle is node 3. Split left-left = -1->5->3, left-right = 4. Sort left-left: Find middle: slow=-1, fast=5. Iteration 1: slow=5, fast=null. Middle is node 5. Split: left-left-left = -1->5, left-left-right = 3. Sort left-left-left: Find middle: slow=-1, fast=5. Middle is node -1. Split: single nodes -1 and 5. Merge: -1->5. Sort left-left-right: single node 3. Merge -1->5 with 3: Compare -1 and 3: -1 smaller. Compare 5 and 3: 3 smaller. Compare 5 and None: add 5. Result: -1->3->5. Sort left-right: single node 4. Merge left-left result (-1->3->5) with left-right (4): Compare -1 and 4: -1 smaller. Compare 3 and 4: 3 smaller. Compare 5 and 4: 4 smaller. Compare 5 and None: add 5. Result: -1->3->4->5. Step 5: Sort right: single node 0. Step 6: Merge left result (-1->3->4->5) with right (0): Compare -1 and 0: -1 smaller. Compare 3 and 0: 0 smaller. Compare 3 and None: add 3, then 4, then 5. Final: -1->0->3->4->5.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Merge Two Sorted Linked Lists
ladders.desc: Merge two already-sorted linked lists into one sorted list
ladders.explanation: This is the most critical helper function in merge sort for linked lists. The intuition is simple: since both lists are already sorted, we can build the merged list by repeatedly taking the smaller of the two current heads, similar to the merge step in merge sort for arrays. The key advantage for linked lists is that we can merge in O(1) extra space by rearranging pointers, unlike arrays which require O(n) additional space. Step-by-step algorithm: 1. Create a dummy node to simplify edge cases. 2. Maintain a current pointer starting at dummy. 3. While both lists have nodes: Compare the values of the two heads. Attach the smaller node to current.next. Move that list's head forward. Move current forward. 4. When one list is exhausted, attach the remaining list to current.next. 5. Return dummy.next as the new head. Time complexity: O(n + m) where n and m are lengths of the two lists. Space complexity: O(1) - we only use a few pointers. Connection to L0: This function is called repeatedly in the merge sort recursion to combine sorted halves. Common mistakes: 1) Forgetting to move pointers forward after attaching nodes, 2) Not handling the case where one list is empty, 3) Creating new nodes instead of reusing existing ones (wastes space), 4) Losing reference to the original heads. Real code pattern: def merge(l1, l2): dummy = ListNode(0); curr = dummy; while l1 and l2: if l1.val <= l2.val: curr.next = l1; l1 = l1.next; else: curr.next = l2; l2 = l2.next; curr = curr.next; curr.next = l1 if l1 else l2; return dummy.next

examples.trace: Step 1: Create dummy node. curr = dummy. Step 2: Compare 1 and 2: 1 smaller. curr.next = node 1. curr moves to node 1. l1 moves to node 3. Step 3: Compare 3 and 2: 2 smaller. curr.next = node 2. curr moves to node 2. l2 moves to node 4. Step 4: Compare 3 and 4: 3 smaller. curr.next = node 3. curr moves to node 3. l1 moves to node 5. Step 5: Compare 5 and 4: 4 smaller. curr.next = node 4. curr moves to node 4. l2 moves to node 6. Step 6: Compare 5 and 6: 5 smaller. curr.next = node 5. curr moves to node 5. l1 becomes null. Step 7: l1 is exhausted, attach remaining l2 (6). curr.next = node 6. Result: dummy.next points to 1->2->3->4->5->6.

Step 1: Create dummy. curr = dummy. Step 2: Compare 2 and 1: 1 smaller. curr.next = node 1. curr moves to 1. l2 moves to 3. Step 3: Compare 2 and 3: 2 smaller. curr.next = node 2. curr moves to 2. l1 moves to 7. Step 4: Compare 7 and 3: 3 smaller. curr.next = node 3. curr moves to 3. l2 moves to 9. Step 5: Compare 7 and 9: 7 smaller. curr.next = node 7. curr moves to 7. l1 moves to 8. Step 6: Compare 8 and 9: 8 smaller. curr.next = node 8. curr moves to 8. l1 becomes null. Step 7: l1 exhausted, attach remaining l2 (9). curr.next = node 9. Result: 1->2->3->7->8->9.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Find Middle of Linked List (Slow/Fast Pointers)
ladders.desc: Efficiently find the middle node of a linked list to split it for divide-and-conquer
ladders.explanation: This is the central technique that enables efficient splitting in merge sort for linked lists. The intuition is brilliant: by moving one pointer twice as fast as another, when the fast pointer reaches the end, the slow pointer will be at the middle. This is essentially measuring distance - if one person walks twice as fast as another, when the fast person finishes the race, the slow person will be exactly halfway. For linked lists, this gives us O(n) time to find the middle without needing to count nodes first. Step-by-step algorithm: 1. Initialize slow = head, fast = head.next (starting fast one step ahead avoids off-by-one errors). 2. While fast is not null and fast.next is not null: Move slow = slow.next (one step). Move fast = fast.next.next (two steps). 3. When loop ends, slow is at the middle (or just before middle for even-length lists). 4. For splitting: middle = slow.next, then set slow.next = null to disconnect. Time complexity: O(n) - fast pointer traverses approximately n/2 steps. Space complexity: O(1) - only two pointers. Connection to L1: This splitting is necessary before we can merge sorted halves - we need to divide the list first. Common mistakes: 1) Starting both pointers at head (causes incorrect middle for even-length lists), 2) Not checking both fast and fast.next in loop condition, 3) Forgetting to disconnect the list after finding middle, 4) Off-by-one errors in determining exact middle point. Real code pattern: def findMiddle(head): if not head: return None; slow = head; fast = head.next; while fast and fast.next: slow = slow.next; fast = fast.next.next; return slow

examples.trace: Step 1: slow = node 1, fast = node 2. Step 2: fast exists and fast.next exists (node 3). Move slow to node 2, fast to node 4. Step 3: fast exists and fast.next exists (node 5). Move slow to node 3, fast to node null (fast.next.next). Step 4: Loop ends because fast is null. slow is at node 3, which is the middle. For splitting: middle = slow.next = node 4, then set slow.next = null to disconnect: left half = 1->2->3, right half = 4->5.

Step 1: slow = node 1, fast = node 2. Step 2: fast exists and fast.next exists (node 3). Move slow to node 2, fast to node 4. Step 3: fast exists but fast.next is null. Loop ends. slow is at node 2. For splitting: middle = slow.next = node 3, then set slow.next = null: left half = 1->2, right half = 3->4. Note: In even-length lists, this gives us the first middle (node 2), which is correct for merge sort splitting.

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursive List Processing
ladders.desc: Understand how recursion works with linked lists and base cases
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is essential for implementing merge sort recursively. The intuition is that linked lists are naturally recursive structures: a list is either empty or consists of a head node followed by another list. This makes recursion a perfect fit. In merge sort, we recursively split the list until we reach base cases (empty list or single node), then build back up by merging. Key insights: 1) The base case for sorting is when the list has 0 or 1 element (already sorted). 2) Each recursive call works on a smaller sublist. 3) The recursion depth is O(log n) for merge sort. Step-by-step thinking: 1. Check base case: if head is null or head.next is null, return head. 2. Otherwise, split the list into two halves. 3. Recursively sort each half. 4. Merge the sorted halves. Time complexity analysis for understanding: Each level of recursion processes all n nodes, and there are log n levels, giving O(n log n). Space complexity: O(log n) for recursion stack. Connection to L2: Recursion uses the middle-finding technique to split lists at each step. Common mistakes: 1) Missing base case leading to infinite recursion, 2) Not properly dividing the problem (splitting incorrectly), 3) Not combining results correctly, 4) Stack overflow for very large lists (though O(log n) depth makes this unlikely). Real code pattern: def recursiveSort(head): if not head or not head.next: return head; mid = findMiddle(head); right = mid.next; mid.next = None; leftSorted = recursiveSort(head); rightSorted = recursiveSort(right); return merge(leftSorted, rightSorted)

examples.trace: Initial call: sort(3->1->2). Not base case (length 3). Find middle: slow/fast gives node 1. Split: left = 3->1, right = 2. Recursive call 1: sort(3->1). Not base case. Find middle: node 3. Split: left-left = 3, right-left = 1. Recursive call 2: sort(3) - base case, returns 3. Recursive call 3: sort(1) - base case, returns 1. Merge 3 and 1: result = 1->3. Back to initial left half sorted: 1->3. Recursive call 4: sort(2) - base case, returns 2. Merge left result (1->3) with right result (2): Compare 1 and 2: 1 smaller. Compare 3 and 2: 2 smaller. Compare 3 and None: add 3. Final: 1->2->3.

Initial call: sort(4->2). Not base case. Find middle: slow=4, fast=2. Middle is node 4. Split: left = 4, right = 2. Recursive call 1: sort(4) - base case, returns 4. Recursive call 2: sort(2) - base case, returns 2. Merge 4 and 2: Compare 4 and 2: 2 smaller, then 4. Result: 2->4.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Traverse and Manipulate Linked List Pointers
ladders.desc: Fundamental skills for navigating and modifying linked lists
ladders.explanation: This is the foundational operation needed for all linked list algorithms. The intuition is that linked lists are chains of nodes where each node points to the next, so manipulation involves carefully updating these pointers without losing access to nodes. Unlike arrays where we have random access, linked lists require sequential access via pointers. Key operations: 1) Traversal: starting at head, follow next pointers until null. 2) Insertion: update pointers to splice in new nodes. 3) Deletion: bypass nodes by updating pointers. 4) Pointer reassignment: changing where nodes point. For merge sort, we need all these skills: traversing to find middle, splitting by reassigning pointers, merging by rearranging pointers. Step-by-step basics: To traverse: curr = head; while curr: process curr.val; curr = curr.next. To insert node after current: newNode.next = current.next; current.next = newNode. To delete node after current: current.next = current.next.next. Time complexity: O(n) for traversal. Space complexity: O(1) for in-place operations. Connection to L3: Recursive processing builds upon these basic pointer manipulations. Common mistakes: 1) Losing reference to head, 2) Creating cycles by incorrect pointer assignments, 3) Accessing null pointers, 4) Not updating all necessary pointers during modifications. Real code pattern: class ListNode: def __init__(self, val=0, next=None): self.val = val; self.next = next; def traverse(head): curr = head; while curr: print(curr.val); curr = curr.next

examples.trace: Step 1: Start with list: node1(1) -> node2(2) -> node3(3). Step 2: Create new node4(4). Step 3: Find node2: traverse from head. curr = node1, then curr = node2. Step 4: Insert: node4.next = node2.next (which is node3). Step 5: node2.next = node4. Result: node1(1) -> node2(2) -> node4(4) -> node3(3). Pointers: node1.next = node2, node2.next = node4, node4.next = node3, node3.next = null.

Step 1: Initial: node5(5) -> node10(10) -> node15(15). Step 2: To delete node10, we need to modify node5's next pointer. Step 3: Traverse to find node5: start at head (node5). Step 4: node5.next = node5.next.next (skip node10). This changes node5.next from pointing to node10 to pointing to node15. Step 5: node10 is now unreachable (garbage collected if no other references). Result: node5(5) -> node15(15).

testCases.input: a -> b -> c, Find node b

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a Linked List?
ladders.desc: Understanding the basic data structure: nodes connected by pointers
ladders.explanation: This is the absolute prerequisite concept. A linked list is a linear data structure where elements (called nodes) are stored in non-contiguous memory locations, unlike arrays which use contiguous memory. Each node contains two parts: 1) data (the value), and 2) a pointer/reference to the next node in the sequence. The intuition is like a treasure hunt where each clue points to the location of the next clue - you must follow the chain sequentially. The first node is called the head, and the last node points to null (indicating end). Key characteristics: 1) Dynamic size (can grow/shrink easily), 2) Efficient insertions/deletions (O(1) if at known position), 3) Sequential access only (no random access), 4) Extra memory for pointers. Types: singly linked (one direction), doubly linked (both directions), circular (last points to first). For our merge sort problem, we use singly linked lists. Visualizing: [data|next] -> [data|next] -> [data|next] -> null. Operations at this level: creating nodes, linking them, traversing from head to null. Time complexity basics: Access: O(n), Search: O(n), Insertion at beginning: O(1), Deletion at beginning: O(1). Connection to L4: All advanced manipulations build on this basic understanding of node-pointer structure. Common beginner mistakes: 1) Confusing nodes with values, 2) Not understanding that 'next' is a pointer/reference, 3) Forgetting to handle null terminators, 4) Not visualizing the chain structure. Real code pattern: class Node: def __init__(self, data): self.data = data; self.next = None; node1 = Node(1); node2 = Node(2); node1.next = node2; # Creates 1 -> 2

examples.trace: Step 1: Create node1 with data=1, next=None. Step 2: Create node2 with data=2, next=None. Step 3: Create node3 with data=3, next=None. Step 4: Link them: node1.next = node2. Now node1 points to node2. Step 5: node2.next = node3. Now node2 points to node3. Step 6: node3.next remains None (end of list). Structure: [1|pointer] -> [2|pointer] -> [3|null]. Head is node1. To traverse: Start at node1, print 1. Follow pointer to node2, print 2. Follow pointer to node3, print 3. Next is null, stop.

examples.input: What is the head of list: 5 -> 10 -> 15?
examples.trace: Step 1: The linked list has three nodes. Step 2: The first node in the chain is called the head. Step 3: In the chain 5->10->15, the node containing 5 is first. Step 4: Therefore, head is the node with value 5. Step 5: From head, we can access entire list: head.next gives node 10, head.next.next gives node 15, head.next.next.next gives null.

testCases.input: How many nodes in null list?
Create single node list with value 7
What marks end of linked list?
Can we directly access third node?
